segment.display_title: Raspberry Pi ethernet connectivity troubleshooting workflow
segment.summary: Troubleshot Raspberry Pi network connectivity issue where device failed to appear in router's connected devices list despite successful ethernet connection with link lights active. Explored multiple diagnostic approaches including router admin page refresh, ARP scanning, mDNS hostname resolution (raspberrypi.local), and verified that stock Raspberry Pi OS and Ubuntu images have ethernet enabled by default. Concluded that user needs a USB microSD card reader to either directly edit WiFi configuration files or reflash the image, as attempting to reset the Pi via SSH or serial console would be unnecessarily complex given the connectivity barrier.

memories.text: Built invokeother_tool for MIRA — a dynamic tool-loading mechanism that pulls tool definitions from an external tool repo on demand rather than baking them into the context window. Considers it easy to build precisely because of the clean infrastructure separation: tools live in a registry, the context window is just a view into what's currently loaded. Finds it notable that no other AI system he's encountered has a similar capability, while other developers are reportedly trying to cram up to 100 tool definitions into a single context window.

Noticed MIRA's response repeated the architectural implication of a user-stated point rather than adding something new, then raised the question of whether the system prompt guideline against mirroring needs sharper language — specifically to distinguish restating-as-echo from restating-as-building. Requested three codeblock variations for the guideline wording.

Has a Raspberry Pi that lost connection to its original WiFi network — troubleshooting via ethernet direct to router but the Pi isn't showing up in connected devices despite green link lights. Doesn't have a microSD card reader to pull and rewrite the SD card from a MacBook Pro, so is considering purchasing a USB card reader as the path of least resistance.
